messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant which rephrase the following text in your own words.
As an AI assistant, I am here to provide assistance and rephrase the following text. Please provide the text you want me to rephrase, and I will be happy to help.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I hear this excuse a lot in podcasting. Whether it's to defend or oppose, some podcasters seem to think that everything a successful podcaster does is directly contributing to their success, and nothing is costing them P.R.O.F.I.T. (popularity, relationships, opportunities, fun, income, or tangibles).

I often come across this argument within the podcasting community. It seems that some podcasters believe that every action taken by a successful podcaster directly leads to their success, without considering the potential trade-offs in terms of popularity, relationships, opportunities, enjoyment, income, and tangible benefits.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Unless we have a parallel universe for split-testing (like in Amazon's The Peripheral), or unless we have a time machine, or unless we can interview every person everywhere with an honest and 100% response rate, or unless we are omniscient like God, we can't really know what is negatively affecting a successful podcast.

Without access to a parallel universe for split-testing, a time machine, the ability to interview every individual with 100% honesty and response rate, or possessing omniscience like a deity, it becomes difficult to definitively identify the factors that may be negatively impacting a successful podcast.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: If that thing actually is negatively affecting their show, you can't actually know! Can you monitor a parallel universe where the only difference is that podcasting thing? Can you travel back in time to have him or her do or not do that thing and then remeasure the results from the present? Can you interview every person everywhere in the world to collect data on whether that thing is affecting that person's relationship with that podcast? Are you God?

It is impossible to truly know whether that particular element is negatively impacting their show. Monitoring a parallel universe where the only variable change is that podcasting factor, time traveling to observe the outcome, interviewing every person worldwide to gather data on the impact of that element on their relationship with the podcast – these actions are unattainable. It is crucial to recognize our limitations and acknowledge that we are not omnipotent like a higher power.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Even if something does contribute to someone else's success, it doesn't mean it will give you the same success. Think about how many “on fire” podcasts there have been that interviewed entrepreneurs. How many of those podcasters got anywhere near the success John Lee Dumas has? How many of those podcasts are even still active?

Simply because something has contributed to someone else's success does not guarantee that it will lead to the same level of success for you. Consider the numerous podcasts that have gained attention by featuring interviews with entrepreneurs—how many of these podcasters have achieved a level of success comparable to John Lee Dumas? Furthermore, how many of these podcasts are still active and thriving today?

Rephrase the following text in your own words: There will always be outliers, too. Marc Maron's show contains excessive profanity—that doesn't mean yours should! Dan Carlin's Hardcore History publishes inconsistently—that doesn't mean you should be inconsistent! No Agenda releases two 3-hour-or-longer episodes every week with multiple, long donation segments—that doesn't mean you should do the same! Plenty of highly popular podcasts focus on controversial moral and political issues from any side you can imagine—that doesn't mean you should include a tangent about them in your podcast! And on and on.

There will always be unique cases that stand out. For instance, Marc Maron's show includes explicit language, but that doesn't imply that you should incorporate excessive profanity into your own podcast. Similarly, Dan Carlin's Hardcore History has an irregular publishing schedule, but that doesn't mean you should follow suit and be inconsistent in releasing episodes. No Agenda may have lengthy episodes with multiple donation segments each week, but that doesn't mean you should feel obligated to do the same. Additionally, many highly popular podcasts delve into controversial moral and political topics from various perspectives, but that doesn't mean you should feel compelled to include tangents about these issues in your own podcast. The examples go on and on, highlighting the importance of recognizing that what works for others may not necessarily work for you.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I'm seeking an instructional resource (aka a link) that I can forward to a podcaster in order to “nudge” them to take action(s) needed in order to allow access [to] ALL their earlier podcast episodes. At present, one can only access [the latest 50 episodes] via an app or via their website ….

I am looking for a resource, preferably a link, that I can share with a podcaster to encourage them to make necessary changes that would enable access to all of their previous podcast episodes. Currently, only the latest 50 episodes can be accessed either through their website or an app.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Many publishing systems will let you limit how many episodes are in your RSS feed (the most-common defaults are 10, 50, and 100), but such limits most likely won't affect your website. If an episode is no longer in your feed but still on your website, it is technically still online and available, but it's no longer very practical because it's not available in your audience's podcast apps. Unless they have smart tools for moving website audio into a podcast app, the only way your audience could hear or watch that old episode is through the webpage and their browser. That makes it difficult for your audience.

Many publishing systems provide options to restrict the number of episodes in your RSS feed (commonly set to 10, 50, or 100), but these limitations typically do not impact the content displayed on your website. If an episode is no longer included in your RSS feed but still exists on your website, it technically remains accessible online, yet it becomes less practical as it is no longer available through your audience's podcast apps. Unless there are specific tools that allow transferring website audio to a podcast app, the only way for your audience to listen to or watch those older episodes is by visiting the webpage and using their browser. This creates difficulties for your audience in accessing the content.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Timeless content is highly valuable for both you and your audience. It gives you more content that you can promote, either through marketing or direct recommendation. For example, I still send people to my episode from 2014 about whether episode numbers are really necessary. Even when I considered reapproaching that content (and ultimately made a completely new episode about episode numbers in titles), I listened to that old episode and realized it was still just as relevant today as back then.

Content that remains relevant over time holds great value for both you as a podcaster and your audience. Such content provides you with additional material that can be promoted effectively, whether through marketing efforts or personal recommendations. For instance, I still direct people to an episode I recorded in 2014, which explores the necessity of using episode numbers. Even though I contemplated revisiting this topic and eventually created a new episode specifically focused on episode numbers in titles, I found that the original episode from 2014 remained just as pertinent and applicable to this day.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: The other kind of content is time-sensitive. It's when your podcast episodes have a kind of “shelf life.” Imagine a “best if used by” date on your episodes. This is common for podcasts about current events (like the news). Podcasts about current trends (such as a TV show) are mostly time-sensitive—because most people will be interested only when that trend is current—but this can still have some timeless value. In the case of a simultaneous TV aftershow podcast, the value might be more difficult as people can more easily binge the show and your episodes will be easily outdated. But if someone gets your podcast while watching the show, as it was back when the show was live, then they could experience it all the same way, except without the direct engagement with your podcast and community. Alternatively, a rewatch-style podcast (such as Office Ladies) weighs more on the timeless side because such a podcast can discuss the show without theorizing or worrying about spoilers, and it's designed generally for people who have already seen the show and not necessarily watching it for the first time.

The other type of content is time-sensitive, meaning that it has an expiration date or a limited relevance period. It's comparable to a "best if used by" date for your podcast episodes. This often applies to podcasts focused on current events, such as news-related topics. Podcasts centered around current trends, like discussing a TV show, also tend to fall into the time-sensitive category. While these episodes may have some timeless value, their primary appeal is usually limited to when the trend or show is currently popular. For instance, in the case of a podcast discussing a TV show's episodes in real-time, the value might diminish as viewers can easily binge-watch the show and the podcast episodes would quickly become outdated. However, if someone discovers your podcast while actively watching the show during its original airing, they can still enjoy the content, albeit without the direct engagement with your podcast or community. On the other hand, podcasts that adopt a rewatch-style format, like "Office Ladies," lean more towards timeless content. These podcasts can delve into discussion and analysis of the show without worrying about spoilers or theorizing about future events. They are designed for an audience that has already seen the show and not necessarily watching it for the first time.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Ask yourself, “Will most of my episodes still be relevant in a year?” If yes, then you probably have timeless content and should consider keeping your episodes available. But if not, it would probably be okay to limit how many episodes are available.

Take a moment to reflect and ask yourself, "Will the majority of my episodes retain their relevance a year from now?" If the answer is yes, then it is likely that you have timeless content and should consider keeping all your episodes accessible. However, if the answer is no, then it would be reasonable to limit the number of episodes available.
